coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of highly water-soluble LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles as anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Longqi
Chen, Cailing
Lv, Zipeng
Xie, Minggang
Yu, Ying
Liang, Chen
Lou, Yue
Li, Chunguang
Shi, Zhan
coredata.dc:description: Highly water-soluble LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles with an average diameter of 165 nm were synthesized by a simple and rapid microwave-assisted hydrothermal method with water as solvent and polyacrylic acid (PAA) as surfactant. The conditions for synthesizing high-quality LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles were screened out by adjusting pH, temperature and the concentration of Eu3+ ions. The solution of high-quality nanoparticles with relatively long fluorescence lifetime showed strong red emissions under ultraviolet excitation. Due to the high water-solubility and good fluorescence property, the aqueous solution of LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles was used as ink for anti-counterfeit printing, which meets various anti-counterfeit requirements.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LuVO4 nanoparticle
Fluorescent ink
Microwave-assisted synthesis
Water-soluble
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The nanoparticles as anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink
Analysis of crystal structure, morphology and fluorescence property of as-prepared nanoparticles
Effects of pH, temperature and Eu3+ doping concentration on LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles
LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles as anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles. Literature values for the peak positions and intensities for bulk LuVO4 samples are indicated by the vertical bars (JCPDS Card No. 17–0880).
TEM image and element maps of LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles.
IR spectra of (a) pure PAA and (b) LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles.
Excitation spectrum (left) and emission spectrum (right) of LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles.

Emission spectra and decay curves of (a-b) LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles obtained at 180 °C, 140 °C and 100 °C (λem = 618 nm, λex = 312 nm) and (c-d) LuVO4:5%Eu, LuVO4:10%Eu, LuVO4:15%Eu and LuVO4:25%Eu nanoparticles, respectively.
Fluorescent images using LuVO4:5%Eu anti-counterfeit ink under 302 nm UV irradiation in dark field: (a) letters, (b) school motto of Jilin University, (c) flower and (d) two-dimensional code.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of highly water-soluble LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles as anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, 2699 Qianjin Street, Changchun 130012, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly water-soluble LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles with an average diameter of 165 nm were synthesized by a simple and rapid microwave-assisted hydrothermal method with water as solvent and polyacrylic acid (PAA) as surfactant. The conditions for synthesizing high-quality LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles were screened out by adjusting pH, temperature and the concentration of Eu3+ ions. The solution of high-quality nanoparticles with relatively long fluorescence lifetime showed strong red emissions under ultraviolet excitation. Due to the high water-solubility and good fluorescence property, the aqueous solution of LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles was used as ink for anti-counterfeit printing, which meets various anti-counterfeit requirements.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of science and technology, there are many fake goods and documents in our daily work and life, which poses a great threat to our personal safety and property [1]. Most of the fake items cannot be judged by appearance, thus prompting us to improve the means of anti-counterfeiting [2]. Among anti-counterfeiting techniques, fluorescent anti-counterfeiting technology is simple and widely used, such as watermarks of passport and paper-based currency notes [3,4]. Compared to traditional dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, the advantages of rare earth nanomaterials as anti-counterfeit ink include high stability, environmental friendliness and good fluorescence [5–8]. Besides, the operation is so easy that it can meet the anti-counterfeiting of small businesses and even individuals [9]. Therefore, rare earth nanomaterials are promising for security and anti-counterfeiting applications [10–12]. Among rare earth nanomaterials, vanadate is a kind of matrix sensitized materials [13,14]. A luminescent process is that the energy absorbed by VO4 3- from excitation light transfers rare earth luminescent ions and then the rare earth ions emit characteristic peaks [15,16]. In general, the energy levels of VO4 3- and Eu3+ are well matched, so that Eu3+ doped rare earth vanadate has good fluorescence properties [17]. Compared to other vanadate, like YVO4 [18] or LaVO4 [13], LuVO4 as an important member of the vanadate family is relatively less studied. Most of the research work about LuVO4 is focused on the evolution of morphology (micrometer size) and tuning of luminescence [19]. However, simple synthesis of highly water-soluble LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles is rarely reported. The advantages of microwave-assisted synthesis include fast heating rate, uniform heating no thermal gradients and good security, so it has become an increasingly important option for fabricating nanomaterials [20].

Herein, we report a green, fast and straightforward microwave-assisted hydrothermal approach to synthesize LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles. We have tuned the synthetic conditions of nanoparticles in detail. The as-prepared LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles possessed highly crystalline structure, high water-solubility and strong fluorescence emission. The aqueous solution of the as-prepared nanoparticles was used as ink for fluorescence anti-counterfeiting.

Polyacrylic acid (PAA, Mw~1800) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Ltd. High purity (99.99%) EuCl3·6H2O and LuCl3·6H2O were supplied by Energy Chemical. Na3VO4·12H2O (Na3VO4 ≥ 45.5%) and NaOH (≥96%) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Ethanol was bought from Beijing Chemical Works.

LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles were prepared by a commercial microwave reactor (ETHOS ONE, ITALY) system. In a typical process, LuCl3·6H2O (0.57 mmol), EuCl3·6H2O (0.03 mmol) and PAA (3.6 mmol) were dissolved in deionized water (9 mL) to form clear and transparent solution A. Na3VO4 (0.6 mmol) was dissolved in deionized water (6 mL) to make a clear and transparent solution B. Then, solution B was injected into solution A. The mixture suddenly became yellow and turbid liquid. After adjusting pH to 12 with 1 M NaOH, the reaction mixture was transferred into a reacting vessel of a microwave reactor, which was sealed and maintained at 180 °C for 10 min. After reaction, a milk-like liquid was obtained. After adding ethanol (10 mL) to precipitate and centrifuge, the obtained product was washed by water and ethanol in sequence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The nanoparticles as anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: Filter paper was selected for writing and painting with LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles ink. Solution of LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles with a certain concentration, like 0.1 mg mL−1, was injected into the cartridge of a commercial inkjet printer to print or filled into a pipe of pen to write. Finally, patterns were excited with a 302 nm UV lamp.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on a Rigaku D/Max 2500 diffractometer (Cu sealed tube: λ = 1.54178 Å, operating voltage: 50 kV, operating current: 200 mA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired on a FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin with a field emission gun operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra were acquired by FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin equipped with an EDX detector. Fluorescence spectra were detected by an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 spectrofluorimeter. Infrared (IR) spectra were characterized on a Bruker IFS 66 v/S FT-IR spectrometer. Digital photos were taken through a Canon 600D camera with EF-s 15–135 mm lens.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of crystal structure, morphology and fluorescence property of as-prepared nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles were synthesized in a solution containing 5 mol% Eu3+ at 180 °C and pH 12 (denoted as LuVO4:5%Eu hereafter). XRD pattern of the as-prepared LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles ( Fig. 1) depicts that the diffraction peak positions and intensities well match with those of bulk LuVO4 specimen, which confirms that the as-prepared nanoparticles have good phase purity and high crystallinity.

Morphology of as-prepared LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles was investigated by transmission electron microscopy ( Fig. 2), which confirms the nanoparticles are relatively uniform and mono-disperse. Based on a statistical analysis, average size of the as-prepared nanoparticles is about 165 nm (see Fig. S1). The high-angle annular dark field scanning TEM (STEM HADDF) image is shown in Fig. 2. By processing the STEM HADDF image, element maps can be obtained (the right row figures of Fig. 2), which confirms the presence and uniform distribution of Lu, V, O and Eu elements in the LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles. Fig. S2 provides a high magnification TEM image of two single LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles. As we can see, surface of the as-prepared nanoparticles is rough, which indicates the nanoparticles are not single crystals. Actually, the LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles are nanoclusters consisting of small primary crystals.

Surfactant plays two vital roles in the synthesis of nanoparticles: i.e. controlling growth and increasing re-dispersibility of nanoparticles. In our experiment, PAA was selected as surfactant. Surface state of as-prepared LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles was characterized by IR spectra ( Fig. 3). There is an absorption peak (Label 1) in the IR spectrum of pure PAA (Fig. 3(a)), which is caused by the CO stretching mode of protonated carboxyl group. Regarding LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles (Fig. 3(b)), labels 2 and 3 are the asymmetric and symmetric C-O stretching modes of the COO- group, respectively. Labels 1–3 demonstrate that a large number of COO- groups were coated on the surface of nanoparticles, which attributes greatly to the high water-solubility oft he nanoparticles.

Fluorescence property is the most important for the study of rare earth nanomaterials [21]. The fluorescent mechanism of rare earth vanadate is host matrix sensitized fluorescence [22–24]. Fig. 4 includes the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles in aqueous solution. The excitation spectrum was recorded under emission at 618 nm, which is ascribed to the absorption of VO4 3- group. Emission spectrum was obtained under 312 nm excitation. Right side of Fig. 4 shows that there are four characterization peaks. The emission bands centered at 594 nm, 618 nm, 652 nm and 700 nm are attributed to the 5D0→7FJ (J = 1, 2, 3, 4) level transitions of Eu3+, respectively. In addition, fluorescence lifetime is a standard indicator for evaluating the fluorescence properties. Fluorescence decay curve of Eu3+ (5D0→7F2, 618 nm) in LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles is depicted in Fig. S3. The lifetime is determined to be 0.934 ms from the decay curve fitted by a single exponential function (I = I0 exp(- t / τ)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH, temperature and Eu3+ doping concentration on LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: In the synthetic process, we have optimized the conditions from a series of experiments. pH is of great importance in the synthesis of vanadates. Fig. S4 is XRD patterns of the products yielded at pH = 6, 9 and 12, respectively. In contrast, LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles cannot be formed when the solution is mildly alkaline (pH = 6). When pH reaches 9, LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles begin to form, but XRD peak intensity is weaker than that of the nanoparticles obtained at pH 12. Meanwhile, as Fig. S5 shows, the fluorescence intensities were gradually enhanced when pH of reaction solution adjusted from 6 to 12 (actually, the fluorescence intensity of products obtained at pH = 6 was hardly to detect.).

Reaction temperature is also a key factor in the synthetic process of nanoparticles. In addition to 180 °C, we also conducted syntheses of LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles at 140 °C and 100 °C, respectively. XRD pattern (Fig. S6(a)) demonstrates LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles can also be prepared at 100 °C. But Fig. S6(b) shows the nanoparticles are not even as before. Moreover, Fig. 5(a) is the fluorescence emission spectrum of LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles prepared at different reaction temperatures. It is obvious that the emission intensity enhances with reaction temperature. Fluorescence lifetimes are in agreement with the emission spectrum. The fluorescence decay curves (Fig. 5(b)) were fitted by a single function to get fluorescence lifetimes (Table S1).

Concentration of luminescent ions is a critical factor to affect the fluorescence intensity of nanoparticles. The concentration of Eu3+ in LaVO4 nanoparticles is systematically investigated. From Fig. S7, we can see that the fluorescence intensity gradually increases when the doping concentration of Eu3+ ions is from 1 mol% to 5 mol%. However, the fluorescence intensity reduces with increasing doping concentration (As showed in Fig. 5(c), the emission intensity reduces when the concentration increases from 5 mol% to 25 mol%). On one hand, with an increase in luminescent centers (Eu3+ ions), emission intensity enhances. On the other hand, the distance between two luminescent centers gets closer, which incurs non-radiative transition easily. Therefore, there will be a proper Eu3+ doping concentration for LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles. It can be concluded that 5 mol% Eu3+ doping concentration would be the optimal. Fig. 5(d) is the fluorescence decay curves of the LaVO4 nanoparticles with 5 mol%, 10 mol%, 15 mol% and 25 mol% Eu3+ doping, respectively. By single exponential function fitting, the lifetimes are shown in Table S1, which coincides with the emission spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles as anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the high water-solubility and strong fluorescence emission, aqueous solution of as-prepared LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles was adopted as fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting demonstration. The aqueous solution of as-prepared nanoparticles was placed into the tube of a pen for hand-writing. Fig. S8 is the image of a piece of paper with LuVO4:Eu fluorescent pattern under daylight, which can be seen that it is just normal paper. However, under UV light the pattern is shown. Fig. S9a and S9b demonstrate some Chinese characters (motto of Jilin university) and a stick figure (cute panda), respectively, which means that this ink can not only be used for writing, but also for painting. Moreover, the ink can be injected into cartridge of printer for printing. The printing process was described in the experimental section and the printing patterns are shown in Fig. 6. Fig. 6(a) are some printed letters, which are clear and bright. Fig. 6(b) is the symbol of our university. From Fig. 6(c), it can be seen that the printed flower has a sense of hierarchy because of the different amounts of jetting ink. Definitely, for improving anti-counterfeiting effect, the common two-dimensional code has been combined with florescent anti-counterfeiting to design the fluorescent two-dimensional code with double anti-counterfeiting functions. Based on that, the aqueous solution can be used as fluorescent ink for anti-counterfeiting, especially for protecting the security of small business company and personal information due to low cost and facile operation.

In summary, a rapid, simple and green microwave-assisted heating hydrothermal method was used to synthesize highly water-soluble LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles. In this process, water and PAA were selected as solvent and surfactant, respectively. Crystal structure, surface state and fluorescence properties of as-prepared LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles were systematically investigated. By optimizing the synthesizing conditions, we identified proper pH (12), temperature (180 °C) and the concentration of Eu3+ ions (5 mol%) to obtain high-quality LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles. The high-quality LuVO4:5%Eu nanoparticles possessed a fluorescence lifetime of 0.934 ms and strong red emission under excitation. The aqueous solution of highly water-soluble LuVO4:Eu nanoparticles with good fluorescence used as anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink can meet various anti-counterfeit requirements.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21621001, 21771084 and 21771077), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFB0701100), the 111 project (No. B17020), the S&T Development Program of Jilin Province of China (No. 20160101325JC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.10.088.
